data.title: Terreno Realty: Buying At Premiums Again
data.description:
In November, I saw mixed signal for shares of Terreno Realty (NYSE:TRNO). The company moved away from a focus on nominal growth towards deleveraging, as it maintained to adhere to its distinguished and successful long-term investment philosophy.

With management issuing more shares than assets being bought with the proceeds, management implicitly gave a signal that shares were richly valued in the low sixties in the fall.

Ever since, shares have come down a bit, driven by the market rout in general, as I am puzzled by fairly aggressive asset purchases announced by year-end 2024. This makes that while appeal might be improving a bit, I am still cautious to get involved here, amidst still demanding FFO multiples, despite a great long-term track record.

A Focus On The Coasts

Terreno focuses on the acquisition, ownership and management of warehouses and associated industrial real estate properties on the West and East coast of the US. This involves cities like Los Angeles, Seattle and the Bay Area on the West coast, and cities and areas like Miami, New York and Washington D.C. on the East coast.

The company is quite agnostic to both core and value-add real estate investment, yet it favors simplicity in terms of investments, which means that it does not (like to) do green fields and joint ventures. Instead, simplicity is favored, combined with buying assets at a discount to replacement values, as well buying assets which are located in very densely populated areas.

The focus is on cities and regions which see supply being constrained, sometimes even being negative. This, and a boom in the economy and inflationary pressures allowed by the business to report double-digit same-store NOI growth in recent years, while occupancy rates continue to come in around optimum levels.

The company posted occupancy rates around 98% for 2024, with the company owning and operating no less than 299 buildings, in total measuring over 19 million square feet.

The Valuations

Terreno grew 2023 rental revenues by 17% to $323 million, driven by a healthy mix of same-store rent growth and asset purchases, with the commonly used FFO metric reported at $2.22 per share, that largely paid out in the form of a $1.80 per share dividend.

The company issued shares in the spring of 2024, with the business using money to acquire properties at cap rates between 5 and 6%, which felt a bit dilutive, as this "conflict" within capital allocation made me a bit cautious. Through the first nine months of the year, the company acquired nearly half a billion worth of assets, yet it sold some three-quarter of a billion of stock in equity issuances, implying a deleveraging of the balance sheet.

Based on 98 million shares trading around the $60 mark, the company commanded a $5.9 billion equity valuation in November of last year, as net debt of $432 million was very modest. With quarterly revenues trending at $100 million, the implied cap rate stood at 6.3%, but this is of course a long way from net operating income generated from such rental activities.

With equity on a net basis being sold, while the company announced some smaller divestments in November, I liked these moves, but still barked at a mid-twenty times FFO metrics, despite a great long-term track record and very strong balance sheet.

What Happened?

Since the fall, shares of Terreno have largely traded in a $50-$65 range, now trading at $57 per share, marking modest losses, perhaps as investors fear the impact of tariffs on some real estate markets in which the company operates.

In the meantime, the company has been quite active on the corporate front. In December of last year, the company announced a massive $156 million purchase of an asset in Brooklyn at a cap rate just below 5%. This was accompanied by another $195 million deal in Florida, bought at a 4.6% cap rate, and another $50 million deal in Queens.

This was followed by some smaller acquisition and divestments announced in the first quarter of the year, yet these are generally much smaller deals, individually valued at less than ten million each.

The company reported 2024 rental revenues at $382 million, up spectacularly from $323 million in the year before, driven by same store rent growth and acquisition activity. That understates the amount of growth, as fourth quarter revenues topped $103 million, even as substantial buying activity only took place by the end of the year.

Adjusted FFO rose by twenty cents to $2.42 per share for the year as the share tally rose to nearly 100 million shares. Net debt ticked up to $811 million, still very manageable in relation to a $6 billion equity valuation.

The company ended the year with a balance sheet of $4.8 billion, yet this included about $400 million worth of deals announced in December of last year which shows up in the balance sheet, but hardly contributes to 2024 rental revenues and FFO.

Shareholder equity on the books stood at $3.7 billion and with nearly 100 million shares representing a $5.7 billion equity valuation, that implies a $2 billion premium compared to the accounting value of equity on the books.

If we add this to $4.7 billion worth of properties, as valued on the balance sheet, we can derive implicit valuation thoughts. This shows that the market is valuing these assets at $6.7 billion, yet with fourth quarter rental revenues trending at $412 million, real rents are shown for this. Assuming roughly $15-$20 million in annual rent from M&A efforts by year-end, the assets are implicitly valued by the market at around 6.5% cap rates.

This again comes as recent acquisition activity took place at a premium multiple (read narrower cap rates) than the own business, but this is supported by a modestly leveraged balance sheet as the company has plans to improve yields over time, while it holds significant development land as well.

What Now?

With FFO having improved meaningfully to $2.42 per share in 2024, multiples on this front have fallen to 23–24 times, still a premium, although that investors obtain a nice 3.4% dividend yield.

By now, the company has delivered on nearly tripling FFO and dividends on a per-share basis over the past decade, with absolute growth coming in higher due to dilution incurred over time. This shows that the business grows in nominal terms, but also growth in a quality fashion, which is very encouraging.

While current multiples are not dirt cheap, the company pays a nice dividend and offers real growth, and while valuations are not cheap, shares have been trading flat since 2020, which implies real valuation multiple compression here.

While this makes me a bit more upbeat, I am still puzzled behind the recent transaction activity. After applauding the company for making some sales in the fall, the company went on an aggressive acquisition spree with cap rates in the 4s in December, sending mixed signals again. Moreover, a 15% exposure to the LA region is concerning as well, given the dismal traffic numbers reported amidst the trade war with China.

At current levels, the dividend provides real support in combination with the great long-term record, yet I wonder if current acquisition activity allows for a continuation of such a great long-term track record at current cap rates, making me cautious to get involved just yet.

data.title: Dell: Buy The Dip At 6-7x Operating Income
data.description:
In February 2023, I wrote an article on Dell (NYSE:DELL), highlighting the company as an attractive buy opportunity "amidst profitability expansion". Since the publication of the article, Dell shares have appreciated by 131%, compared to a gain of 38% for the S&P 500 (SP500).

Today, I see another attractive investment opportunity with Dell, as shares have lost 18% YTD. Core to the thesis is Dell's continuing, strong earnings story, which is hard to ignore: In FY 2025, Dell generated close to $7 billion of EBIT. And in FY 2026, Dell is expected to grow operating income by 25–30% YoY, to $9.1 billion. Notably, if estimates are correct, then shares are effectively trading at 6x operating income, well below the 15–25x operating income multiple seen in other tech businesses. I reiterate a "Buy" rating. My target price is around $128.

Dell Is Riding The Tailwind Of The AI Growth Story

Dell has been, and continues to be, a key beneficiary of the AI infrastructure build-out. Investors should note that Dell has a deep and strategic partnership with NVIDIA, centering on integrating NVIDIA’s high-performance GPUs, such as the H100 and upcoming Blackwell architecture, into Dell's PowerEdge servers. This gives Dell a leading position in delivering to clients optimized platforms for AI training, inference, and large-scale data processing. And accordingly, as Nvidia sees high order intakes on the backdrop of increasing computing demands, Dell rides the tailwind alongside Nvidia.

Concerns Around Slowing AI Demand...

Since the beginning of the year, there have been some concerns that the demand for Nvidia chips is fading, or turning weaker than hoped. This concern caused Dell shares to drop quite substantially from all-time highs. Most recently, Super Micro Computer's (SMCI) disappointing earnings warning and a substantial $1.4 billion writedown added another layer of concern relating to a slowing AI infrastructure backdrop. Notably, SMCI announced on April 30, 2025, that its third-quarter revenue would fall to $4.5–$4.6 billion, well below its prior guidance of $5–$6 billion. As a reason for this revision, SMCI management cited delays in customer AI server orders.

...Not Yet Reflecting In Fundamentals

However, with the given information, it is not clear whether SMCI's stumble could actually create opportunities for Dell (and others such as HPE) to capture a larger share of the AI server market. For instance, I highlight that xAI reportedly shifted $6 billion in AI server orders from SMCI to Dell amid SMCI’s accounting issues, highlighting Dell’s potential to gain from competitors' setbacks.

Moreover, it is important to note that the concern surrounding fading AI infrastructure build-out has so far not been reflected in fundamentals. Indeed, analyst consensus estimates for Dell revenue in FY 2026 have only dropped by about $1–2 billion, since shares hit an all-time highs in Q2 2024.

More notably still, I highlight that revenue expectations for Dell's Infrastructure Solutions Group have actually increased since Q2 2024, by about $4 billion, to nearly $51 billion. And estimates are still trending upwards. Thus, the weakness in Dell's fundamentals won't likely be found in the company's AI narrative.

For context, in Q4 FY 2025, Dell's revenue from Infrastructure Solutions Group jumped 22% YoY, to $11.4 billion.

Expect $9+ Billion Of EBIT In FY 2026

As of calendar week 19, the big hyperscale tech giants (Meta Platforms, Google, Microsoft, Amazon) have reported earnings for Q1 2025. And as expected, none of the companies have voiced intentions to slow down AI infrastructure CAPEX as compared to earlier projections. This, in my view, should bolster investor confidence about the AI infrastructure investment cycle until at least Q2 reporting.

On profitability, analyst consensus, according to data collected by Refinitiv, currently expects Dell's FY 2026 EBIT at around $9.1 billion. If correct, this would suggest a 28% YoY jump vs FY 2025, and an operating margin expansion of about 180 bps.

Benchmarking analysts' estimates on Dell's FY 2026 EBIT against the company's market cap highlights that Dell shares are effectively trading at around 6-7x operating income.

Valuation With Notable Upside

Zooming in on a potential valuation estimate for Dell, and building on consensus expectations through 2027, I am revising my residual earnings model for Dell: For CY 2025, CY 2026, and CY 2027, I now project EPS of $9.3, $10.4, and $11, respectively. At the same time, I set a 10% cost of equity and a 2% terminal growth input. With these inputs, my updated base case target price increases to approximately $128, well above the current trading price of around $94 (+36% upside).

Below, readers may also find the updated sensitivity table for varying assumptions on the cost of equity (rows) and terminal growth rates (columns).

Investor Takeaway

I view Dell stock as a clear "buy", with shares down 18% YTD and trading at "laughably low" 6-7x FY 2026 operating income—well below the 15-25x multiples investors would pay when investing in tech heavyweights. Despite the noise about fading AI demand triggered, amongst others, by Super Micro Computer's $1.4 billion stumble, Dell's fundamentals remain solid: Recent earnings reports from the big hyperscale tech companies clearly suggest that the AI infrastructure tailwind continues to persist. On that note, analysts see FY 2026 Infrastructure Solutions Group revenue now at $51 billion, a $4 billion bump since Q2 2024; Dell group FY 2026 EBIT is expected at $9.1 billion, up 28% YoY. My updated residual earnings model, using conservative EPS forecasts ($9.3, $10.4, $11 for CY 2025-2027), a 10% cost of equity, and 2% terminal growth, calculates a $128 base case target price.

data.title: Dell: Betting On Servers, Not Screens
data.description:
Dell Technologies Inc. (NYSE:DELL) is a value play at reasonable prices. The growth stagnation, particularly in its PC business (Client Solutions Group or CSG), is priced into the valuations (we will evaluate in greater detail). The upside potential remains from AI infrastructure growth.

With a track record of shareholder paybacks and an ability to participate in the AI-led upside, Dell's investment thesis masks over a lack of recurring revenue to cheer about compared to a SaaS or AI company, heavy competition, and even a perceived innovation gap. Or does it?

A Look at the Financials

First of all, the revenue stagnation is not really that bad. In fact, from early 2024, Dell has seen a turnaround in revenue growth, clocking above 5% YoY. Most of the turnaround is based on its AI infrastructure-led revenue growth in ISG (Infrastructure Solutions Group), while CSG revenues have held on without significant erosion. Guidance for 2026 is robust at 8% growth YoY.

Margins are not concerning either. Again, through 2024, the margins have actually improved. The operating margin looks healthy enough to retain Dell's pricing power and input cost pressures in a tariff regime. The gross margins have benefitted heavily from workforce efficiencies and reflect Dell's emphasis on cost control. Guidance is mostly flat for 2026 as the product mix shifts further to AI-optimized servers and Dell keeps heavy competition in check in a tariff-inflicted regime.

Free cash flow is concerning. Since Dell has a reasonable debt demand to service and woos investors through paybacks, a drop in free cash flow is not encouraging. A lot of the free cash flow impact is due to strategic investments in building capability for AI infrastructure (and also some temporary operation working capital issues). The payout ratio is still reasonable at 22% and does not threaten dividends. The core leverage ratio of 1.2 also shows reasonable control over its debt exposure while balancing free cash flows for capex investments and shareholder paybacks ($1.1b returned to investors through repurchases and dividends).

The strategic investments are already showing decent traction. Dell's AI orders demand was $1.7b in Q4 2025, and the reported backlog looks healthy and increasing at a good pace – Dell exited Q4 2025 with a backlog of $4.1b, and by February 2025, that grew to $9b on the back of key partnerships with xAI and others.

The R&D expenses increased by 9% in 2025, and that is offset by a 7% drop in general and administration expenses. Dell is upping its R&D spend and financing it through workforce efficiencies. Despite the shareholder payouts, Dell ended the year with a cash and investments balance of $5.2b. While it's not a cause for alarm, I am cautious about the projected 10% annual dividend increase for the next few years. Also, I would have liked to see a higher R&D or capex breakup of investments, as Dell transitions into the AI infrastructure space. Either the shareholder returns will be deprioritized, or investments may be inadequate in a heavily competitive and fast-changing AI segment.

AI as the Key Lever

I am bullish about AI's total addressable market, and Dell is already on its way to mainstream server market share. With a gain of 7% market share in the past 10 years, Dell is way ahead of the competition in the space. Dell is projected to clock $15b in AI shipments in FY 2026, up by 53%, as per guidance. Traction and growth levels in the ISG business show a great start to the AI hyper-growth journey.

The risks to its continued growth from AI infrastructure demand are that hyperscalers designing and building their own infrastructure at some point. The complex ecosystem with NVIDIA is also a risk as NVIDIA is capable of building its own AI infrastructure or prioritizing Supermicro, leading to a short supply of NVIDIA GPU for Dell. GPU/CPU architecture is also likely to evolve in the AI infrastructure journey, and today's leadership could be disrupted very easily if Dell (or anyone in this space) does not adapt fast and strategically throughout the new regime. Having said so, the risks to AI growth are applicable to all players in the field, and Dell's superior positioning at the moment shows good enough green shoots to bet on. Of course, investors will need to keep a close tab on tech developments as they evolve.

Valuation

Compelling from a historical averages' perspective, but not so much in comparison to peers. Despite combining the CSG and ISG businesses, unlike what is seen in peers, Dell's forward PE is even ahead of HPE (which has a strong case to benefit from the AI hyper-growth). I assume the premium is factoring in Dell's leadership position and higher margin enterprise solutions than, say, HPE's capex-heavy legacy hardware.

Impact from Tariffs

Dell's case is impacted significantly by tariffs both on the importing of components for manufacturing from China and selling of its consumer products through exports. Dell is working on supply chain restructuring to enable manufacturing lines from Mexico and Vietnam. But as of date, short-term input cost pressure will impact margins or sales, depending on how Dell absorbs the cost increase. From an end market perspective, I am less concerned as Dell's more important and growing area is AI infrastructure, where margins are higher to absorb costs or even pass on partially to end enterprise (less price sensitive than the PC laptop customer).

Buy and Be Ready to Accumulate

With similar prospects in AI-led growth, I had given a buy call for HPE. That call was helped by the steep discounts available for entry at that point. The recent rebound in Dell prices makes it a harder buy with tariff impacts looming.

Dell's margins are great, and the business is set to gain from the AI growth, potentially more than HPE in absolute terms. However, I am concerned about the cash flow and future investments required to support growth in the relatively new AI segment, particularly as Dell prioritizes shareholder paybacks. Valuations appear just about fair to cheap, but factor in the tariff impact on its significant base of the CSG business, and doubts creep in.

All in all, a 10-15% correction from here would have been a "more perfect" time to enter (taking it closer to the tariff sell-off lows). But you cannot time the markets, particularly if you are bullish long term. I recommend a "Buy" on Dell because of its long-term prospects. Investors are advised to keep cash as a buffer to buy on further dips to ride over the tariff concerns.
